title: Kitchen herb sachet
subtitle: A muslin sachet of dried culinary herbs for scenting kitchen drawers and cupboards
excerpt: A small sachet of dried culinary herbs (rosemary, thyme, and bay leaf) placed in a kitchen drawer or cupboard provides a subtle herbal scent and deters moths and silverfish naturally. The same herbs used in cooking make the sachet entirely food-safe for use near cutlery, napkins, or stored ingredients. Refresh annually.
sourceNotes: Dried rosemary, thyme, and bay as drawer sachets: traditional household aromatic craft. Rosemary and bay as insect deterrents: volatile compounds (camphor, eucalyptol, linalool) in dried Rosmarinus officinalis and Laurus nobilis have documented deterrent effects against common textile pests and pantry insects: essential oil repellency studies, Journal of Stored Products Research. All ingredients are food-grade herbs; no safety concerns for use near food storage.

recipe.batchNotes: Make a batch of 4 to 6 sachets at once. They make good gifts.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make sachets in advance; they keep for up to 12 months in a sealed bag before use, which concentrates the fragrance.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Keeps 12 months in a drawer or cupboard. When the fragrance fades, crumble the herbs gently through the bag to release more of the volatile compounds. Replace annually or when the herbs no longer have a noticeable scent.

glossaryTerms.definition: Aromatic chemicals that evaporate at room temperature and are responsible for the scent of herbs and essential oils. As dried herbs release these compounds slowly over time, they provide a low-level, long-lasting fragrance.

content.text: Dried rosemary, thyme, and bay release

slowly at room temperature, giving a gentle herbal scent that lasts months. In a kitchen drawer or cupboard, these sachets also deter pantry moths and silverfish. The herbs are standard cooking ingredients, so the sachets are food-safe next to cutlery, linens, or stored dry goods.

items.prepNote: main herb; provides a strong camphor-like scent
crumble lightly before adding to the bag to release more scent; roughly 6 to 8 leaves
adds a floral sweetness; omit for a purely savoury herb blend

content.text: Lightly crumble the bay leaves to break them up slightly; this exposes more surface area. Combine all the herbs and fill 4 small muslin drawstring bags, about 2 tablespoons of herb mix per bag. Tie or pull the drawstring closed. Label if you plan to store some before use.

Place one sachet in each drawer or cupboard shelf. Kitchen drawers (cutlery, linen napkins), the back of a pantry shelf, or a cupboard storing dry goods are all suitable locations. Do not place directly in contact with foods stored loose without packaging.
Sachets keep 12 months in a drawer. When the fragrance fades, press or crumble the bag gently between the fingers to release more volatile compounds. Replace annually.
